name: War
description: Stories in which armed conflict is central, emphasizing combat, strategy, survival, and the human cost of violence and duty.

nodes.label: War Promise
nodes.definition: The audience is promised a story in which armed conflict is the central engine — one that will not flinch from the physical, psychological, and moral costs of war, while tracing the bonds forged and broken under extreme violence and duty.
entry_conditions: Story is positioned as war genre
exit_conditions: Core constraints are established that fulfill this promise
typical_variants: Combat front-line war
Home front war
Anti-war narrative
Military strategy and command
failure_modes: War setting used purely as backdrop for unrelated personal drama
Combat presented as exciting spectacle without cost or consequence
signals_in_text: Armed conflict is introduced early and drives plot
Human stakes of violence are established from the outset
Protagonist is embedded in a military or wartime structure

nodes.label: Armed Conflict Must Be Central
nodes.definition: The story's central plot engine must be armed conflict — organized, collective violence between opposing forces. War is not background atmosphere; it directly determines what characters do, what they risk, and what the outcome means.
exit_conditions: Armed conflict drives the main plot and cannot be removed without collapsing the story
typical_variants: Direct front-line combat
Strategic command decisions with battlefield consequences
Partisan or guerrilla conflict
Siege and occupation
failure_modes: War is setting but the central conflict is personal rivalry unrelated to combat
Battles occur off-page with no narrative weight
signals_in_text: Combat operations drive plot decisions
Opposing forces are defined and active
Outcome of the war shapes the story's resolution

nodes.label: Human Cost of Violence Must Be Visible
nodes.definition: The war genre demands that the price of violence be made visible — death, wounding, grief, psychological damage, civilian suffering, and moral injury must register on the narrative, not be elided. The audience must feel what the conflict costs.
exit_conditions: Death and injury carry weight; characters mourn, are damaged, and bear the marks of what they have done and witnessed
typical_variants: Physical casualties among named characters
Psychological breakdown or moral injury in survivors
Civilian and collateral cost shown
Long-term trauma carried beyond the battlefield
failure_modes: Enemies die in masses without registering as people
Protagonist kills without psychological effect
Wounded recover instantly and conveniently
signals_in_text: Named characters die and are mourned
Violence leaves visible physical and psychological marks
Collateral damage is shown, not glossed over

nodes.label: Duty vs. Survival Tension
nodes.definition: War stories require a persistent tension between the demands of duty — to mission, to command, to comrades — and the individual's drive to survive. Characters must navigate orders that may get them killed, loyalty that demands sacrifice, and survival instinct that pulls against obligation.
entry_conditions: Character is embedded in a military or wartime structure
exit_conditions: At least one character faces a meaningful choice between duty and survival, and this choice carries consequences
typical_variants: Following a suicidal order vs. retreating
Staying to cover comrades vs. escaping
Obeying immoral commands vs. refusing and facing punishment
Sacrificing self to complete the mission
failure_modes: Duty and survival never conflict — orders are always safe and reasonable
Tension is raised but avoided rather than confronted
signals_in_text: Characters debate or internally wrestle with orders
Scenes where following orders risks death
Consequences for those who refuse or desert

nodes.label: Brotherhood and Unit Cohesion Under Pressure
nodes.definition: The bonds formed within a fighting unit — trust, loyalty, shared suffering, and collective identity — must be a living element of the story. Unit cohesion is tested by fear, loss, command failure, and moral collapse; its presence or fracture shapes the war narrative's emotional core.
entry_conditions: Protagonist is part of a military unit or wartime group
exit_conditions: Unit relationships are developed and tested; the state of unit cohesion has narrative consequences
typical_variants: Tight squad bonds forged in first combat
Unit fracture under unbearable loss
Cross-cultural bonds between unlikely comrades
Loyalty tested by cowardice or betrayal within the group
failure_modes: Soldiers are interchangeable and unnamed
Unit exists only as backdrop — no interpersonal dynamics
Bonds form and hold without meaningful pressure or rupture
signals_in_text: Named comrades with distinct roles and personalities
Interpersonal conflict and reconciliation within the unit
Death of a comrade registers as significant loss

nodes.label: Combat/Front-Line War
nodes.definition: War stories set on or near the active battlefield, where the protagonist is a combatant and the primary experience is direct engagement with enemy forces — the noise, fear, chaos, and intimacy of front-line fighting.
entry_conditions: Protagonist is an active combatant in direct contact with the enemy
exit_conditions: Story centers on combat operations at the point of contact — patrols, assaults, defenses, ambushes
typical_variants: Infantry squad on patrol
Assault on fortified positions
Defensive last stand
Amphibious or airborne operation
failure_modes: Combat is choreographed and bloodless
Front-line setting but protagonist is never in real danger
Tactical reality is ignored — enemies cooperate with the plot
signals_in_text: Scene-level tactical detail of engagements
Sensory immediacy of combat — noise, confusion, fear, chaos
Protagonist makes life-and-death decisions under fire

nodes.label: Home Front War
nodes.definition: War stories centered on those not fighting at the front — civilians, families, workers in war industries, resistance members, refugees — whose lives are transformed, threatened, and defined by the war they experience from a distance or in occupation.
entry_conditions: Protagonist is a non-combatant or rear-area figure whose life is shaped by the war
exit_conditions: Story dramatizes how war reshapes civilian life — scarcity, loss, propaganda, occupation, moral compromise
typical_variants: Occupied city resistance
Family waiting for a soldier's return
War worker or nurse in proximity to the front
Refugee survival narrative
failure_modes: Home front is insulated from war's reality — no scarcity, fear, or loss
Civilian protagonist has no meaningful agency in relation to the war
signals_in_text: War's material effects on daily life are shown
Protagonist makes dangerous choices because of the war's demands
Loss arrives through news, casualties, or direct violence

nodes.label: Anti-War Narrative
nodes.definition: War stories structured to interrogate, condemn, or expose the futility, horror, and institutional failure of war — using the war genre's conventions to argue against war rather than celebrate it, often through irony, tragedy, or the systematic destruction of martial idealism.
entry_conditions: Narrative stance interrogates or condemns the war rather than affirming its necessity or heroism
exit_conditions: Story produces disillusionment, tragedy, or moral indictment as its dominant effect
typical_variants: Satirical anti-war comedy exposing absurdity
Tragedy of the idealistic soldier ground down by institutional indifference
Survival story where victory is hollow
Moral injury narrative — protagonist wins militarily but loses morally
failure_modes: Anti-war stance is stated but not dramatized — characters say war is bad but the story glorifies it
Irony is so thick the human cost disappears into abstraction
signals_in_text: Institutional authority is shown as indifferent or corrupt
Heroism is undercut by futility or senselessness
Survivors carry wounds that make victory feel like defeat

nodes.label: Military Strategy and Command
nodes.definition: War stories centered on the officers, commanders, and planners who shape the war at the operational or strategic level — where the drama is in decisions made in headquarters, the cost of those decisions for those who execute them, and the moral weight of sending others to die.
entry_conditions: Protagonist operates at a command or planning level, making decisions that affect many lives
exit_conditions: Story dramatizes the gap between strategy and execution, the costs of command decisions, and the moral burden of authority
typical_variants: Senior officer managing a campaign
Intelligence officer shaping operations
Political-military interface — civilian and military authority in conflict
Failed commander reckoning with consequences
failure_modes: Command decisions are made without visible human cost to those who execute them
Strategy is a puzzle game — no ethical weight to decisions about lives
signals_in_text: Protagonist sees the map but not always the battlefield
Decisions made in this node have consequences shown in combat nodes
Moral burden of command is dramatized

nodes.label: Battlefield Geography Shapes Action
nodes.definition: The physical landscape of war — terrain, weather, fortifications, lines of supply, chokepoints — must actively shape what characters can and cannot do. Geography is not backdrop; it determines tactical reality and therefore determines plot.
exit_conditions: Geography creates tactical constraints that characters must work with or against — cover, elevation, water obstacles, urban terrain
typical_variants: Jungle or forest limiting visibility and movement
Urban rubble as cover and obstacle
Open ground as killing field
Mountain passes as chokepoints
River crossings as tactical crises
failure_modes: Combat occurs in featureless space — terrain is never described or used tactically
Characters move freely regardless of terrain logic
signals_in_text: Terrain is described with tactical implication
Characters use or are constrained by landscape features
Weather and ground conditions affect mission outcomes

nodes.label: Chain of Command Constrains Individual Action
nodes.definition: Military hierarchy — the chain of command — must function as a structural constraint on individual characters. Orders come from above, they must be obeyed or disobeyed with consequences, and the gap between command intent and battlefield reality is a permanent source of dramatic tension.
entry_conditions: Protagonist is within a military or paramilitary structure
exit_conditions: Chain of command is visible, creates obligations, and generates tension when orders conflict with circumstances or conscience
typical_variants: Competent officer given impossible order by incompetent superior
NCO interpreting orders to protect his squad
Soldier ordered to do something immoral
Command breakdown under fire — initiative forced on individuals
failure_modes: Characters act with total autonomy regardless of military context
Orders are never questioned, refused, or problematic
Hierarchy exists on paper but has no narrative function
signals_in_text: Orders are issued and the drama of compliance or refusal follows
Rank and authority shape who speaks and who obeys
Consequences for disobedience or command failure are shown

nodes.label: Scarcity and Danger Are Constant
nodes.definition: The war environment must be characterized by persistent scarcity — ammunition, food, medical supplies, sleep, information, time — and persistent danger. Characters operate without safety nets. Resources are never automatically replenished and safety is never assumed.
exit_conditions: Scarcity creates decision pressure; danger is background-constant rather than intermittent
typical_variants: Ammunition rationing under fire
Medical supplies insufficient for the wounded
Food and water deprivation on extended operations
Sleep deprivation degrading judgment
Information scarcity — the fog of war
failure_modes: Characters have unlimited supplies throughout
Danger only appears during designated action sequences — between them, all is safe
Wounds are treated immediately with full resources available
signals_in_text: Characters track remaining ammunition or supplies
Medical treatment is improvised or delayed
Characters make errors due to exhaustion or incomplete information

nodes.label: Into Combat Scene
nodes.definition: An early scene in which the protagonist enters combat for the first time in this story, establishing the sensory reality of the war — the chaos, the fear, the physical and psychological experience of being under fire and returning it. This scene sets the war's baseline of danger and human cost.
exit_conditions: Audience has experienced combat through the protagonist — not just observed it from a distance; the war's reality is viscerally established
typical_variants: Ambush that initiates the squad into the real war
Assault on a position that reveals the chaos of battle
First artillery bombardment establishing helplessness under fire
First firefight where the protagonist must kill
failure_modes: First combat is shown in summary or from a distance
Protagonist enters combat without fear or difficulty
Combat scene is exciting but cost-free — no casualties, no psychological effect
signals_in_text: Sensory detail of combat — sound, smell, confusion, physical fear
Protagonist's interiority during first engagement
Cost of the scene — someone is hurt or killed, something is lost

nodes.label: Loss/Cost-of-War Scene
nodes.definition: A scene dedicated to registering the human cost of war — a named character's death, a devastating wound, a moment of moral injury, the destruction of something irreplaceable. This scene forces the audience and protagonist to reckon with what the war takes. It cannot be glossed over or recovered from quickly.
entry_conditions: Combat has occurred and bonds have been established
exit_conditions: A significant and irreversible loss has been dramatized; grief, guilt, or horror is shown without deflection or quick recovery
typical_variants: Death of a central comrade in the protagonist's arms
Protagonist committing an act that causes civilian casualties
Soldier breaking down under accumulated trauma
Return to what was destroyed — village, home, people
failure_modes: Loss is mentioned but not shown or felt
Death of a named character passed over without grief
Scene rushes to action before loss is absorbed
Loss is reversed or recovered from too quickly
signals_in_text: Narrative slows to register the moment of loss
Protagonist's grief, guilt, or horror is given space
Loss changes something permanently — the unit, the protagonist's worldview, the mission

nodes.label: Mission Climax / Final Stand
nodes.definition: The climactic military sequence in which all prior tension — the mission's objective, the unit's cohesion, the duty-vs.-survival tension, the accumulated cost — is brought to a decisive confrontation. The outcome resolves the central military question of the story, though not necessarily with victory or survival.
entry_conditions: All prior constraints and escalation have built toward this point
The mission's stakes are at maximum
exit_conditions: The central military objective is resolved — achieved, failed, or rendered meaningless — and the human cost of that resolution is visible
typical_variants: Final assault on the objective under full enemy resistance
Last stand defense until relief or until it breaks
Command decision that determines the battle's outcome
Moral climax — refusal to follow an immoral order at the crucial moment
failure_modes: Climax is resolved by luck or by a new resource not previously established
Final stand is anticlimactic — enemy yields too easily
Human cost of the climax is not registered
Climax resolves military question but ignores the human question
signals_in_text: Maximum danger and resource depletion
Every prior element — skills, relationships, moral choices — is brought to bear
Outcome is earned by prior development, not convenience

nodes.label: Gravity and Visceral Intensity
nodes.definition: War demands a sustained tone of gravity — the weight of violence, duty, loss, and moral consequence must be felt throughout. Visceral intensity means the writing does not flinch from the physical and psychological reality of combat and its aftermath. Neither flippant nor numbing — the tone keeps the cost legible.
exit_conditions: Prose or narration maintains the seriousness of war's reality without becoming exploitative or numbing
typical_variants: Unflinching realism in combat description
Elegiac tone in aftermath scenes
Ironic gravity in anti-war subgenre
Procedural seriousness in command/strategy subgenre
failure_modes: Tone is consistently light or comedic without ironic purpose
Violence is described in aestheticizing language that distances its horror
Gravity collapses into sentimentality — grief without complexity
signals_in_text: Combat is described with physical specificity, not abstraction
Aftermath of violence receives narrative attention
Characters' interior responses to war are shown, not suppressed

nodes.label: Glorified Violence Without Human Cost
nodes.definition: War fails as a genre when violence is aestheticized, celebrated, or treated as inherently redemptive without registering the human cost that makes it meaningful. Combat becomes action spectacle; enemies die without humanity; the protagonist kills without consequence. The genre's core promise — that war's cost will be visible — is betrayed.
entry_conditions: Combat or violence occurs in a war context
exit_conditions: Violence carries human cost — injury, grief, moral weight — making glory impossible without complexity
typical_variants: Combat sequences where enemies die in satisfying waves with no human weight
Protagonist kills without psychological impact across the whole story
War framed as adventure or game — danger without consequence
Victory scenes that celebrate without acknowledging what was lost to achieve them
failure_modes: This node IS the failure mode — its presence indicates a genre violation
signals_in_text: No named enemy deaths register as human
Protagonist experiences no lasting psychological effect from killing
Casualties are numerical, not personal
Battle scenes are exciting but no one is diminished by them

edges.label: Promise requires armed conflict as the central engine
edges.effects_on_stakes: Defines that all plot movement must pass through or be shaped by armed conflict
edges.effects_on_character: Characters' choices, losses, and growth are all filtered through the war
common_alternatives: Some war stories subordinate combat to home-front drama, but conflict remains structurally central
anti_patterns: War as backdrop — story could exist without it

edges.label: Promise requires visible human cost of violence
edges.effects_on_stakes: Every act of violence must carry weight — the war's price is always visible
edges.effects_on_character: Characters are changed, damaged, or destroyed by what they do and witness
common_alternatives: Cost may be physical, psychological, or moral — but at least one register must be continuous
anti_patterns: Violence is spectacular but consequence-free

edges.label: Promise requires duty-vs-survival tension
edges.effects_on_stakes: The question of what obligation demands and what survival requires creates permanent dramatic pressure
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must choose between self-preservation and larger obligations, and bear the cost of those choices
common_alternatives: Tension may be external (orders given) or internal (self-imposed standards of honor)
anti_patterns: Duty and survival never conflict — war is convenient and safe for the protagonist

edges.label: Promise requires brotherhood and unit cohesion as a living element
edges.effects_on_stakes: Unit bonds give meaning to sacrifice and loss; their rupture is a major dramatic event
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist's identity is partly constituted by their unit; comrades are not interchangeable
common_alternatives: Solo war stories may replace unit cohesion with a single defining relationship
anti_patterns: Soldiers are unnamed and fungible — no bonds formed or broken

edges.label: Promise requires gravity and visceral intensity as baseline tone
edges.effects_on_stakes: Tone ensures the audience feels the weight of what is at stake — death, duty, loss
edges.effects_on_character: Characters operate under the moral and physical seriousness that war demands
common_alternatives: Anti-war subgenre may use irony or dark comedy, but gravity remains the target — the humor cuts rather than deflects
anti_patterns: Consistently light or adventure-serial tone that treats war as fun

edges.label: Armed conflict branches into front-line combat subgenre
preconditions: Protagonist is an active combatant in direct contact with the enemy
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes are immediate and physical — life and death in the next moment
edges.effects_on_character: Character is shaped by the sensory and psychological experience of direct combat
anti_patterns: Front-line setting but protagonist is never in real danger or combat

edges.label: Armed conflict branches into home front subgenre
preconditions: Protagonist experiences war from a non-combat position
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes are survival, resistance, loss of loved ones, moral compromise under occupation or deprivation
edges.effects_on_character: Character must navigate a war they did not choose and cannot fight directly
anti_patterns: Home front is insulated from war's consequences

edges.label: Armed conflict branches into anti-war narrative subgenre
preconditions: Narrative stance interrogates or condemns rather than affirms the war
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes include the meaning of sacrifice — if the war is futile or wrong, what do deaths mean?
edges.effects_on_character: Character's martial idealism is tested and often destroyed; survival may feel like failure
anti_patterns: Anti-war stance stated but story structure still glorifies combat

edges.label: Armed conflict branches into military strategy and command subgenre
preconditions: Protagonist operates at command or planning level
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes are operational — the outcome of a campaign, the lives of many determined by one decision
edges.effects_on_character: Character bears the moral weight of authority — they send others to die and must live with the consequences
anti_patterns: Command decisions made without visible human cost to those who execute them

edges.label: Front-line combat requires battlefield geography to shape action
edges.effects_on_stakes: Terrain determines who lives and dies — high ground, cover, chokepoints are life-or-death matters
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must read and use terrain; tactical intelligence determines outcomes
anti_patterns: Combat in featureless space — terrain never described or used

edges.label: Front-line combat requires chain of command as structural constraint
edges.effects_on_stakes: Orders from above shape what the squad must do, regardless of local conditions
edges.effects_on_character: Squad leader mediates between command above and soldiers below; NCOs interpret orders to protect their people
common_alternatives: Breakdown of command — protagonist forced into autonomous decision-making
anti_patterns: Soldiers act with total autonomy regardless of military structure

edges.label: Home front requires scarcity and danger as constant conditions
edges.effects_on_stakes: War's material reality — rationing, bombing, occupation, loss of breadwinners — shapes every choice
edges.effects_on_character: Civilian characters must improvise and sacrifice; comfort and safety are not assumed
common_alternatives: Rural area stripped of resources for the front
anti_patterns: Home front civilian life is comfortable and undisrupted by the war

edges.label: Anti-war narrative requires chain of command as the mechanism of institutional failure
edges.effects_on_stakes: Command authority sends people to die for reasons that are shown to be futile, corrupt, or absurd
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist is caught between an institution that demands compliance and a conscience that cannot accept the cost
common_alternatives: Bureaucratic indifference rather than individual evil
Systemic failure rather than command villain
anti_patterns: Anti-war narrative where military authority is wise and just — futility has no institutional cause

edges.label: Military strategy subgenre requires chain of command as the protagonist's primary environment
preconditions: Military strategy and command subgenre selected
edges.effects_on_stakes: Protagonist issues orders that determine others' fates; protagonist also receives orders from political or strategic authority above
edges.effects_on_character: Character is the chain of command — their moral weight is the weight of those who obey them
common_alternatives: Coalition command with competing national interests
anti_patterns: Commander has total autonomy with no accountability upward or downward

edges.label: Front-line combat requires scarcity and persistent danger
edges.effects_on_stakes: Running low on ammunition, medical supplies, or cover transforms tactical situations into survival crises
edges.effects_on_character: Characters manage resources under fire; wounds accumulate and cannot always be treated
common_alternatives: Jungle patrol scarcity — weight limits everything carried
anti_patterns: Unlimited ammunition and supplies — characters never make resource decisions under fire

edges.label: Battlefield geography generates the conditions for the first combat scene
edges.effects_on_stakes: The specific terrain determines how the first combat unfolds — ambush in jungle, assault across open ground, fighting through rubble
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist's first combat experience is shaped by the specific geographic reality of their war
common_alternatives: Open-ground assault as baptism of fire
anti_patterns: First combat occurs in generic, non-specific space with no terrain logic

edges.label: Chain of command issues the order that sends protagonist into first combat
preconditions: Chain of command established as structural reality
edges.effects_on_stakes: The order to go establishes that duty will send characters into danger regardless of personal readiness
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist enters first combat under orders — this is not their choice but their obligation
common_alternatives: Protagonist is thrust into combat before orders can arrive — initiation is forced, not commanded
anti_patterns: Protagonist chooses to enter first combat entirely of their own volition with no institutional context

edges.label: Scarcity and persistent danger generate the conditions of loss
preconditions: Scarcity and constant danger established as setting conditions
edges.effects_on_stakes: When danger is constant and resources are insufficient, loss is inevitable — the cost-of-war scene is the consequence of these conditions
edges.effects_on_character: Characters lose comrades, capacity, and innocence because the conditions of war make survival imperfect
common_alternatives: Loss from moral failure under pressure
anti_patterns: Danger is constant but nobody is ever actually harmed in a way that matters

edges.label: Entry into combat leads to the first significant loss
preconditions: Bonds with comrades have been established
edges.effects_on_stakes: Combat's cost is made concrete through a specific, irreversible loss that the audience has been prepared to feel
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist's experience of first combat's aftermath — not just the battle itself but what it took — defines their war
common_alternatives: Loss may come in the same scene as combat or in its immediate aftermath
Loss may be delayed to a subsequent engagement
anti_patterns: Combat scenes occur but nobody significant is lost or damaged — the war has no cost for the protagonist

edges.label: Accumulated loss shapes the moral weight of the mission climax
preconditions: Prior loss scenes have established what the war has cost
edges.effects_on_stakes: The climax is weighted by everything already paid — the mission's success or failure is measured against what it has cost
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist carries the accumulated cost of prior losses into the final stand — it shapes what they are willing to do and why
common_alternatives: Climax is an act of revenge for prior loss
Climax is an act of redemption for prior moral failure
Climax asks whether the final cost is worth what was already paid
anti_patterns: Mission climax proceeds as if prior losses never occurred — no emotional or moral continuity

edges.label: Duty-vs-survival tension reaches its decisive point at the mission climax
preconditions: Duty-vs-survival tension has been established and sustained throughout
edges.effects_on_stakes: The climax is the moment when the tension must be resolved — duty demands the final stand; survival may counsel retreat
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist must make their definitive choice between obligation and self-preservation, and live or die with it
common_alternatives: Protagonist chooses survival and must reckon with the cost
anti_patterns: Mission climax resolves without the duty-vs-survival tension being engaged

edges.label: Unit cohesion or its fracture determines the character of the mission climax
preconditions: Unit bonds have been developed and tested throughout
edges.effects_on_stakes: A cohesive unit at the climax fights differently than a broken one; the state of brotherhood is a direct determinant of the final stand's character
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist's bonds with comrades are tested to their limit — who stands with them, who breaks, who dies for whom
common_alternatives: Climax framed by the dissolution of the unit
Single surviving member carries the fallen
anti_patterns: Mission climax occurs without any meaningful engagement of unit relationships

edges.label: Failure to make human cost visible produces glorified violence
preconditions: Violence occurs but its human cost is not registered
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes become meaningless — combat is spectacle, not consequence
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist is implicitly endorsed in their violence rather than humanized through its cost
anti_patterns: This edge IS the anti-pattern — it describes what to avoid
